title: JavaScript Intro
content:
JavaScript Introduction

The Language of the Web

JavaScript is the programming language of the web. While HTML defines the structure and CSS defines the style, JavaScript makes web pages interactive.



  • Calculate, manipulate, and validate data
  • Update both HTML and CSS dynamically
  • Show or hide content based on user actions
  • Send and receive data from APIs

title: Where JS Is Used
content:
Where JavaScript Is Used

Beyond the Browser

JavaScript was originally built to run only in web browsers, but today it works on many platforms and environments!

  • Web Browsers: Client-side code running on the user's computer.
  • Web Servers: Backend architecture using environments like Node.js.
  • Mobile Apps: Cross-platform frameworks like React Native.
  • Browser Extensions: Customizing the web browsing experience.

title: JavaScript Output
content:
JavaScript Output

Displaying Data

JavaScript does not have any built-in print or display functions, but you can output data in different ways depending on where you want it to show up.

Output Examples
<p id="output-demo"></p>

<script>
  // 1. Write directly to the HTML Document
  document.getElementById('output-demo').innerHTML = 'Hello from JavaScript!';
  
  // 2. Log to the developer console (Press F12 to see this!)
  console.log('This is a secret message for developers.');
  
  // 3. Trigger a browser pop-up
  // alert('Welcome to the course!'); 
</script>

title: JavaScript Syntax
content:
JavaScript Syntax

The Rules of JS

JavaScript code is made of statements executed one after another. It is case-sensitive (LET is not the same as let) and uses specific syntax rules.

Syntax Basics
<p id="syntax-out"></p>

<script>
  // Statements should end with a semicolon
  let x = 5;
  let y = 6;
  
  // JavaScript ignores extra spaces
  let sum = x + y;
  
  document.getElementById('syntax-out').innerText = "The sum is: " + sum;
</script>

title: JS Comments
content:
Comments

Documenting Your Code

Comments are entirely ignored by the JavaScript engine. They are used to explain complex logic, leave notes for other developers, or temporarily disable code while testing.

Comment Syntax
<script>
  // This is a single-line comment.
  let active = true;
  
  /* 
    This is a multi-line comment.
    It can span across several lines,
    which is great for detailed explanations.
  */
  
  // document.getElementById('hide-me').style.display = 'none';
</script>

title: Variables
content:
Variables

Storing Data

Variables are containers for storing data values. In modern JavaScript, you should use let for values that will change, and const for values that should never be reassigned. Avoid using var unless working with old code.

Variable Declarations
<p id="var-out" style="font-size: 18px;"></p>

<script>
  const name = 'Sanjith'; // Cannot be changed later
  let age = 20;           // Can be updated!
  
  age = 21; // It is Sanjith's birthday!
  
  document.getElementById('var-out').innerText = name + " is now " + age + " years old.";
</script>

title: Data Types
content:
Data Types

Types of Values

JavaScript variables can hold many data types. To understand how to manipulate data, you must first know its type.



  • Numbers: Numeric values (e.g., 10, 3.14)
  • Strings: Text wrapped in quotes (e.g., "Hello")
  • Booleans: True or False logic (e.g., true)
  • Undefined: A variable declared but not assigned a value.
  • Null: An intentionally empty value.
Checking Types
<p id="type-out"></p>

<script>
  let text = "I am a string";
  let num = 42;
  let isActive = false;
  
  // The typeof operator tells you what type of data a variable holds
  document.getElementById('type-out').innerHTML = 
    typeof text + "<br>" + typeof num + "<br>" + typeof isActive;
</script>

title: Operators
content:
Operators

Math and Logic

Operators perform operations on values and variables.

  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (remainder)
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=
  • Comparison: ==, === (strict equal), !=
  • Logical: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)
Operator Magic
<p id="op-out"></p>

<script>
  let x = 10;
  x += 5; // Exactly the same as: x = x + 5;
  
  // The + operator is also used to join (concatenate) strings together!
  let greeting = "Hello " + "World!";
  
  document.getElementById('op-out').innerHTML = "Math: " + x + " | String: " + greeting;
</script>

title: Functions Basics
content:
Functions

Reusable Blocks

Functions are blocks of code designed to perform a particular task. They help organize code, avoid repetition, and make programs easier to read.

Writing a Function
<p id="func-out"></p>

<script>
  // Define the function and what inputs (parameters) it needs
  function greet(name) {
    return 'Hello, ' + name + '! Welcome to JavaScript.';
  }
  
  // Execute (call) the function
  let message = greet('Sanjith');
  
  document.getElementById('func-out').innerText = message;
</script>

title: JS Events
content:
Events

Reacting to Users

HTML events are "things" that happen to HTML elements. JavaScript can "react" to these events to create interactive buttons, forms, and games.

Click Event
<h3 id="event-title">Hover or Click!</h3>

<!-- We can put JavaScript directly inside HTML event attributes! -->
<button 
  onclick="document.getElementById('event-title').innerText = 'You clicked me!'" 
  onmouseover="this.style.backgroundColor='yellow'" 
  onmouseout="this.style.backgroundColor='white'">
  Interactive Button
</button>

title: Strings
content:
Strings

Text Manipulation

Strings are text values enclosed in single or double quotes. JavaScript provides built-in properties and methods to manipulate strings effortlessly.

String Methods
<p id="str-out"></p>

<script>
  let text = "JavaScript is Awesome";
  
  let length = text.length; // Property
  let upper = text.toUpperCase(); // Method
  let cut = text.slice(0, 10); // Method
  
  document.getElementById('str-out').innerHTML = 
    "Original: " + text + "<br>" +
    "Length: " + length + "<br>" +
    "Uppercase: " + upper + "<br>" +
    "Sliced: " + cut;
</script>

title: Numbers
content:
Numbers

Math Operations

Unlike other programming languages, JavaScript only has one type of number. Numbers can be written with or without decimals.

The Math Object
<p id="num-out"></p>

<script>
  let integer = 10;
  let float = 3.14;
  
  // The Math object gives you advanced math powers
  let rounded = Math.round(4.6);
  let randomNum = Math.floor(Math.random() * 10) + 1;
  
  document.getElementById('num-out').innerHTML = 
    "Rounded 4.6: " + rounded + "<br>" +
    "Random Number (1-10): " + randomNum;
</script>

title: Arrays Basics
content:
Arrays

Lists of Data

Arrays are special variables that can hold more than one value at a time. They are ordered, meaning you access the data inside them using an index number (starting at 0).



Array Indexing
<p id="arr-out"></p>

<script>
  // Arrays use square brackets []
  let cars = ['Saab', 'Volvo', 'BMW'];
  
  // Arrays are zero-indexed!
  let firstCar = cars[0]; // Gets 'Saab'
  
  // You can easily change values
  cars[1] = 'Tesla';
  
  document.getElementById('arr-out').innerHTML = 
    "First Car: " + firstCar + "<br>" +
    "Second Car is now: " + cars[1];
</script>

title: Objects Basics
content:
Objects

Real World Entities

Objects are collections of named values called properties. If a variable is a box holding a single value, an object is a filing cabinet holding many related folders.

Object Syntax
<p id="obj-out"></p>

<script>
  // Objects use curly braces {}
  let person = { 
    firstName: 'Sara', 
    lastName: 'Smith',
    age: 25 
  };
  
  // Access properties using dot notation
  document.getElementById('obj-out').innerText = 
    person.firstName + " " + person.lastName + " is " + person.age;
</script>

title: DOM Manipulation
content:
DOM Manipulation

Changing HTML

The Document Object Model (DOM) is an API that represents your HTML page as a tree. JavaScript uses it to find, change, add, or delete HTML elements dynamically.

Selecting Elements
<div id="card" style="padding:20px; border:1px solid black;">
  <h3 id="card-title">Old Title</h3>
  <p class="desc">This is some text.</p>
</div>

<script>
  // Find element by ID and change content
  document.getElementById('card-title').innerHTML = 'New Dynamic Title!';
  
  // Find element by ID and change CSS styles
  document.getElementById('card').style.backgroundColor = 'lightblue';
</script>

title: Conditions
content:
Conditions

Making Decisions

Conditional statements control flow. They allow your program to perform different actions based on different conditions, just like making decisions in real life.

If/Else Statement
<p id="cond-out" style="font-weight:bold;"></p>

<script>
  let score = 85;
  let result = '';
  
  if (score >= 90) {
    result = 'A - Excellent!';
  } else if (score >= 70) {
    result = 'B - Passed!';
  } else {
    result = 'F - Try again.';
  }
  
  document.getElementById('cond-out').innerText = "Score: " + score + " | " + result;
</script>

title: Loops
content:
Loops

Repeating Code

Loops repeat a block of code multiple times. They are especially useful for iterating through the data inside arrays.



The For Loop
<ul id="loop-out"></ul>

<script>
  let listHTML = '';
  
  // for (start condition; run condition; increment)
  for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    listHTML += '<li>Counting: ' + i + '</li>';
  }
  
  document.getElementById('loop-out').innerHTML = listHTML;
</script>

title: ES6 Basics
content:
ES6 Features

The Modern Standard

ECMAScript 2015 (commonly known as ES6) was a massive update to JavaScript that introduced modern syntax developers use every day. If you see code using let, const, or arrow functions (=>), you are looking at ES6!

title: Classes Basics
content:
Classes

Object Blueprints

Introduced in ES6, Classes are templates for creating objects. They encapsulate data with code to work on that data, making Object-Oriented coding much easier in JS.

Basic Class
<p id="cls-out"></p>

<script>
  class Car {
    constructor(brand) {
      this.brand = brand;
    }
    honk() {
      return this.brand + " goes Beep!";
    }
  }
  
  const myCar = new Car('Toyota');
  document.getElementById('cls-out').innerText = myCar.honk();
</script>

title: Basics Summary
content:
JavaScript Basics Summary

Foundation Built!

You now understand the fundamental building blocks of JavaScript. You know how to declare variables, write logic with if/else statements, run loops, and manipulate the HTML DOM.

Let's continue to the next section where we will dive deeper into advanced arrays, scoping, and asynchronous programming!

title: DOM Basics
content:
JavaScript HTML DOM

The Document Object Model

The Document Object Model (DOM) is a tree representation of the HTML page. JavaScript can access and change HTML elements using DOM methods like getElementById and querySelector.



DOM Manipulation
<p id="demo">Waiting for JavaScript...</p>

<script>
  // Select the element and change its text content!
  document.getElementById('demo').innerHTML = 'Hello World! The DOM is working!';
  document.getElementById('demo').style.color = '#00ffb3';
</script>

title: DOM Events
content:
DOM Events

Handling Interactions

Events are actions that occur in the browser — like clicks, mouse movements, and key presses. Use addEventListener() to trigger JavaScript functions when these events happen.

Event Listener
<button id="btn" style="padding:10px 20px; background:#1e293b; color:white; border:none; border-radius:4px; cursor:pointer;">
  Click Me!
</button>
<p id="evt-out" style="margin-top: 15px; font-weight: bold;"></p>

<script>
  document.getElementById('btn').addEventListener('click', function() {
    document.getElementById('evt-out').innerHTML = 'Button clicked! You triggered an event!';
  });
</script>

title: Functions
content:
Functions in JavaScript

Reusable Blocks

Functions are reusable code blocks designed to perform a particular task. Functions can take inputs (parameters) and send back outputs (return values).

Basic Function
<p id="func-out" style="font-size: 20px; font-weight: bold;"></p>

<script>
  function sum(a, b) {
    return a + b;
  }
  
  // Call the function and output the result to the page
  let result = sum(3, 4);
  document.getElementById('func-out').innerText = "3 + 4 = " + result;
</script>

title: Function Expressions
content:
Function Expressions

Anonymous Functions

Functions can be stored in variables. These are often called anonymous functions (functions without a name) and are useful for callbacks and modular code.

Expression Syntax
<p id="expr-out"></p>

<script>
  const greet = function(name) {
    return 'Hello ' + name + '!';
  };
  
  document.getElementById('expr-out').innerText = greet('Sanjith');
</script>

title: Arrow Functions
content:
Arrow Functions

Modern Syntax

Arrow functions provide a concise syntax for writing function expressions. They also behave differently with the this keyword compared to regular functions.

Arrow Function Example
<p id="arrow-out"></p>

<script>
  // Concise, single-line arrow function
  const add = (x, y) => x + y;
  
  document.getElementById('arrow-out').innerText = "5 + 6 = " + add(5, 6);
</script>

title: JS Objects
content:
JavaScript Objects

Key-Value Pairs

Objects store multiple key-value pairs in a single variable. You can add, modify, or delete properties, and even assign functions as properties (methods).

Object Methods
<p id="obj-out"></p>

<script>
  const user = {
    name: 'Sara',
    age: 25,
    sayHi: function() {
      return 'Hi, my name is ' + this.name;
    }
  };
  
  document.getElementById('obj-out').innerText = user.sayHi();
</script>

title: Constructors
content:
Constructor Functions

Object Blueprints

Constructor functions are used to create multiple objects with the exact same structure. Prototypes let you share methods between all instances of that object.



Using 'new' Keyword
<p id="const-out"></p>

<script>
  // Constructor function (capitalized by convention)
  function Person(name, age) {
    this.name = name;
    this.age = age;
  }
  
  const p1 = new Person('Alex', 30);
  const p2 = new Person('Jordan', 28);
  
  document.getElementById('const-out').innerText = p1.name + ' is ' + p1.age + ' years old.';
</script>

title: JS Arrays
content:
JavaScript Arrays Advanced

Lists of Data

Arrays store ordered lists of values. JavaScript has many powerful built-in array methods like map, filter, reduce, and forEach to manipulate lists.

Array Methods
<ul id="arr-out"></ul>

<script>
  let nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];
  nums.push(5); // Adds 5 to the end
  
  let listHTML = '';
  // Loop through the array and build list items
  nums.forEach(n => {
    listHTML += '<li>Number: ' + n + '</li>';
  });
  
  document.getElementById('arr-out').innerHTML = listHTML;
</script>

title: ES6 Features
content:
JavaScript ES6+

Modern JavaScript

ES6 introduced modern JavaScript features that make code cleaner and shorter.

  • let and const: Block-scoped variable declarations.
  • Template literals: Easier string formatting using backticks.
  • Destructuring: Extracting values from arrays or objects effortlessly.
  • Spread and Rest (...): Expanding or grouping iterables.
Destructuring & Templates
<p id="es6-out"></p>

<script>
  const coords = [45, 90];
  const [lat, lng] = coords; // Destructuring the array!
  
  // Template literals using backticks
  document.getElementById('es6-out').innerText = `Your location is Lat: ${lat}, Lng: ${lng}`;
</script>

title: JS Classes
content:
JavaScript Classes

Object-Oriented Syntax

Classes are templates for objects. Introduced in ES6, they provide a much cleaner, more standard Object-Oriented syntax over the traditional Constructor functions.

Class Example
<p id="class-out"></p>

<script>
  class Car {
    constructor(name, year) {
      this.name = name;
      this.year = year;
    }
    getDetails() {
      return this.year + ' ' + this.name;
    }
  }
  
  const car1 = new Car('Ford', 2015);
  document.getElementById('class-out').innerText = "Car Details: " + car1.getDetails();
</script>

title: Scope & Hoisting
content:
Scope & Hoisting

Variable Visibility

Scope defines where variables are accessible (Global vs Block scope). Hoisting is JavaScript's default behavior of moving variable declarations to the top of their scope before code execution.



Scope Error Example
<p id="scope-out" style="color:red;"></p>

<script>
  function runTest() {
    let x = 10; // x is scoped only to this function!
  }
  
  try {
    // Trying to read x outside the function will cause a reference error
    document.getElementById('scope-out').innerText = x;
  } catch (err) {
    document.getElementById('scope-out').innerText = "Error caught: " + err.message;
  }
</script>

title: Closures
content:
Closures

Preserving State

A closure gives a function access to variables from an outer function, even after that outer function has returned. It is widely used for creating private variables and stateful functions.



Closure Counter
<button onclick="updateCounter()">Increment Counter</button>
<h3>Count: <span id="closure-out">0</span></h3>

<script>
  // outer() runs once and returns the inner function
  function outer() {
    let count = 0; 
    return function() { 
      return ++count; // Inner function remembers 'count'
    };
  }
  
  const increment = outer();
  
  function updateCounter() {
    document.getElementById('closure-out').innerText = increment();
  }
</script>

title: Promises
content:
JavaScript Promises

Asynchronous JavaScript

Promises handle asynchronous operations (like fetching data from a server). A Promise represents a value that may be available now, or in the future, or never.



Promise Execution
<h3 id="prom-out">⏳ Waiting for promise...</h3>

<script>
  let myPromise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    // Simulate a network delay of 1.5 seconds
    setTimeout(() => resolve('✅ Done! Promise Resolved.'), 1500);
  });
  
  myPromise.then(result => {
    document.getElementById('prom-out').innerText = result;
  });
</script>

title: DOM Methods
content:
JavaScript DOM Methods

Interacting with Elements

The DOM API lets you dynamically create, find, and change HTML elements using built-in methods.

  • getElementById(): Selects a single element.
  • querySelector(): Selects elements using CSS selectors.
  • createElement(): Creates a brand new HTML element.
  • appendChild(): Adds an element to the page.
Creating Elements Live
<div id="container" style="padding:15px; border:2px dashed #00ffb3;"></div>

<script>
  const container = document.getElementById('container');
  
  // Create a new element from scratch
  const newEl = document.createElement('h3');
  newEl.innerText = 'I was created by JavaScript!';
  newEl.style.color = '#0ea5e9';
  
  // Attach it to the page
  container.appendChild(newEl);
</script>

title: Best Practices
content:
JavaScript Best Practices

Clean Code Standards

Writing code that the computer understands is easy. Writing code that humans understand is hard. Follow these conventions:

  • Avoid Global Variables: They can be overwritten by other scripts and cause bugs.
  • Always Declare Variables: Always use let or const.
  • Use Meaningful Names: let userAge = 25; is much better than let x = 25;.
  • Modularize: Keep your functions small. One function should do exactly one thing.

title: let & const
content:
ES6: let & const

Block Scoping

ES6 introduced block-scoped variables using let and constants using const. These replaced the old var keyword, which often caused confusing scope bugs.

Constants in Action
<p id="lc-out"></p>

<script>
  let mutableScore = 10;
  const MAX_SCORE = 100;
  
  mutableScore += 5; // This is fine!
  // MAX_SCORE = 110; // Un-commenting this will crash the script!
  
  document.getElementById('lc-out').innerText = "Score: " + mutableScore + " / " + MAX_SCORE;
</script>

title: Template Literals
content:
Template Literals

Advanced Strings

Template literals use backticks (`) rather than quotes to define a string. They allow for multi-line strings and easy string interpolation using ${expression}.

String Interpolation
<p id="tl-out"></p>

<script>
  const name = 'Sanjith';
  const role = 'Developer';
  
  // No more messy + signs!
  const greeting = `Welcome back, ${name}. Your role is: ${role}.`;
  
  document.getElementById('tl-out').innerText = greeting;
</script>

title: Destructuring
content:
Destructuring

Unpacking Data

Destructuring assignment allows you to quickly extract specific values from arrays, or properties from objects, and assign them directly into distinct variables.

Object Destructuring
<p id="destruct-out"></p>

<script>
  const user = { username: 'Sara99', age: 25, country: 'India' };
  
  // Extract 'username' and 'country' directly into new variables
  const { username, country } = user;
  
  document.getElementById('destruct-out').innerText = `${username} is from ${country}`;
</script>

title: Spread & Rest
content:
Spread & Rest Operators

The ... Operator

The ... syntax does two opposite things. Spread expands an array into its individual elements. Rest collects multiple elements and condenses them into a single array.



Spread Example
<p id="spread-out"></p>

<script>
  const part1 = [1, 2, 3];
  const part2 = [4, 5, 6];
  
  // Spread expands the old arrays into a brand new combined array
  const combined = [...part1, ...part2];
  
  document.getElementById('spread-out').innerText = `Combined array: [${combined}]`;
</script>

title: The 'this' Keyword
content:
The this Keyword

Execution Context

In JavaScript, the this keyword refers to an object. But which object it refers to depends on how and where this is being called.



  • In an object method, this refers to the object.
  • Alone, this refers to the global window object.
  • In an event, this refers to the HTML element that received the event.
Method Context
<p id="this-out"></p>

<script>
  const person = {
    name: 'Sanjith',
    greet: function() {
      // 'this' knows to look inside the 'person' object for the name
      return "Hello, I am " + this.name;
    }
  };
  
  document.getElementById('this-out').innerText = person.greet();
</script>

title: Call, Apply, Bind
content:
Call, Apply, Bind

Controlling 'this'

Sometimes you need to borrow a method from one object and use it on another. You can explicitly force the this keyword to point to a specific object using these built-in function methods.

The call() Method
<p id="cab-out"></p>

<script>
  const person1 = { 
    fullName: function() { return this.firstName + " " + this.lastName; } 
  };
  
  const person2 = { firstName: "John", lastName: "Doe" };
  
  // person2 borrows the fullName method from person1!
  const result = person1.fullName.call(person2);
  
  document.getElementById('cab-out').innerText = result;
</script>

title: IIFE
content:
Immediately Invoked Functions

Self-Executing Code

An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is a JavaScript function that runs as soon as it is defined. It is a design pattern primarily used to avoid polluting the global namespace.

IIFE Syntax
<p id="iife-out"></p>

<script>
  // Wrapping the function in parenthesis tells JS it is an expression,
  // and the final () executes it immediately!
  (function() {
    let secret = 'I ran immediately without being called!';
    document.getElementById('iife-out').innerText = secret;
  })();
</script>

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling

try / catch

When your code throws an error (an exception), it will normally crash the entire script. The try...catch statement allows you to "catch" the error, handle it gracefully, and keep the application running.

Catching Exceptions
<p id="err-out" style="color: #fca5a5; background: #7f1d1d; padding: 10px; border-radius: 4px;"></p>

<script>
  try {
    // Attempting to call a function that doesn't exist!
    nonExistentFunction(); 
  } catch (err) {
    // Catch the error and print a friendly message instead of crashing
    document.getElementById('err-out').innerText = 'Application Error Saved: ' + err.message;
  }
</script>

title: Async / Await
content:
Async / Await

Syntactic Sugar

The async and await keywords make asynchronous code (Promises) look and behave a little more like synchronous code. This makes reading and maintaining network requests significantly easier.



Async Example
<p id="async-out">Waiting...</p>

<script>
  // 'async' ensures the function returns a Promise
  async function deliverMessage() {
    return "Async/Await is awesome!";
  }
  
  // You can use .then() to wait for the result
  deliverMessage().then(result => {
    document.getElementById('async-out').innerText = result;
  });
</script>

title: Fetch API
content:
Fetch API

Network Requests

The Fetch API provides a modern, Promise-based way to request data across a network. It is the standard way JavaScript applications communicate with backend databases and 3rd party APIs.



Live Data Fetch
<button onclick="getUser()">Fetch User from API</button>
<p id="fetch-out" style="margin-top: 10px; color: #00ffb3;"></p>

<script>
  async function getUser() {
    document.getElementById('fetch-out').innerText = "Loading...";
    
    // Make an actual HTTP request to a public testing API
    const response = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1');
    const data = await response.json();
    
    document.getElementById('fetch-out').innerText = `Success! Fetched User: ${data.name} (${data.email})`;
  }
</script>

title: JSON Data
content:
JSON Parse & Stringify

Data Transfer Format

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the standard format for sending data over the web. Because data must be sent as text, we use JSON.stringify() to convert JS Objects into text, and JSON.parse() to turn received text back into JS Objects.

JSON Conversion
<p id="json-out"></p>

<script>
  // 1. A normal JavaScript Object
  const myObj = { name: "John", age: 31, city: "New York" };
  
  // 2. Convert to a JSON Text String (ready to be sent over a network)
  const myJSON = JSON.stringify(myObj);
  
  // 3. Convert back into a JavaScript Object
  const parsedObj = JSON.parse(myJSON);
  
  document.getElementById('json-out').innerText = "Stringified: " + myJSON;
</script>

title: LocalStorage
content:
LocalStorage

Browser Memory

The localStorage object allows you to save key/value pairs in the browser. The data is saved across browser sessions, meaning the data is still there even if the user closes and re-opens the tab.

Saving Data
<button onclick="saveColor()">Save Color</button>
<button onclick="loadColor()">Load Color</button>
<p id="storage-out" style="margin-top:10px; font-weight:bold;"></p>

<script>
  function saveColor() {
    localStorage.setItem('themeColor', 'Blue');
    alert('Color Saved to browser memory!');
  }
  function loadColor() {
    const saved = localStorage.getItem('themeColor');
    document.getElementById('storage-out').innerText = saved ? `Loaded: ${saved}` : 'No color saved yet!';
  }
</script>

title: Date & Time
content:
Date & Time

The Date Object

JavaScript provides a built-in Date object to work with dates and times. By default, JavaScript will use the browser's time zone and display a date as a full text string.

Date Formatting
<p id="date-out"></p>

<script>
  const now = new Date();
  
  const fullString = now.toString();
  const humanReadable = now.toDateString();
  const justTime = now.toLocaleTimeString();
  
  document.getElementById('date-out').innerHTML = 
    `<b>Raw:</b> ${fullString} <br><br> <b>Formatted:</b> ${humanReadable} at ${justTime}`;
</script>

title: Regular Expressions
content:
Regular Expressions

Pattern Matching

A regular expression (Regex) is a sequence of characters that forms a search pattern. They are incredibly powerful for searching, replacing, and validating strings (like checking if an email is formatted correctly).

Regex Search
<p id="regex-out"></p>

<script>
  const text = "Visit CareerPath to learn coding!";
  
  // Search for the word 'CareerPath' (case-insensitive flag 'i')
  const pattern = /careerpath/i;
  const result = pattern.test(text);
  
  document.getElementById('regex-out').innerText = 
    `Does the string contain our target? ${result}`;
</script>

title: Course Summary
content:
JavaScript Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the comprehensive JavaScript Masterclass. You now have the programming foundation required to build modern, dynamic web applications.

  • Core Fundamentals: Variables, Functions, Arrays, Objects, and Logic.
  • DOM Manipulation: Selecting elements, handling user Events, and updating HTML/CSS dynamically.
  • Modern ES6+: Destructuring, Spread Operators, Classes, and Arrow Functions.
  • Asynchronous JS: Promises, Async/Await, and fetching live data from APIs.

With HTML, CSS, SQL, and JavaScript complete, you have fully built your technical skillset. You are officially ready to start building full-stack applications!

title: DOM Traversal
content:
DOM Traversal

Navigating the Tree

DOM traversal means navigating up, down, and sideways between HTML elements in the DOM tree using their relationship to one another.



Traversal Properties
<div id="parent" style="border:2px solid gray; padding:10px;">
  <p id="child1">First Child</p>
  <p id="child2">Second Child</p>
</div>
<p id="trav-out" style="color:#0ea5e9; font-weight:bold;"></p>

<script>
  const startNode = document.getElementById('child1');
  
  // Traverse to sibling and parent
  const sibling = startNode.nextElementSibling.innerText;
  const parentTag = startNode.parentNode.tagName;
  
  document.getElementById('trav-out').innerText = 
    `My sibling is '${sibling}' and my parent is a '${parentTag}' tag.`;
</script>

title: Create & Remove
content:
Creating and Removing Elements

Dynamic UI

JavaScript can dynamically create brand new HTML elements from scratch, attach them to the page, or remove existing ones based on user interaction.

Append and Remove
<button onclick="addBox()">Add Box</button>
<button onclick="removeBox()">Remove Box</button>
<div id="container" style="display:flex; gap:5px; margin-top:10px; flex-wrap:wrap;"></div>

<script>
  const container = document.getElementById('container');
  
  function addBox() {
    const box = document.createElement('div');
    box.style = "width:50px; height:50px; background:#00ffb3;";
    box.className = "dynamic-box";
    container.appendChild(box);
  }
  
  function removeBox() {
    const boxes = document.getElementsByClassName('dynamic-box');
    if(boxes.length > 0) boxes[0].remove(); // Remove the first box found
  }
</script>

title: Event Delegation
content:
Event Bubbling & Delegation

Smart Event Handling

When you click an element, that event "bubbles" up to its parent, and its parent's parent. Event Delegation uses this behavior to attach a single event listener to a parent element to handle clicks on multiple children (even children created dynamically later!).



Delegation Example
<ul id="myList" style="background:#1e293b; padding:20px; color:white;">
  <li>Item 1 (Click me)</li>
  <li>Item 2 (Click me)</li>
</ul>
<p id="del-out"></p>

<script>
  // We attach ONE listener to the UL, not individual listeners to each LI
  document.getElementById('myList').addEventListener('click', function(event) {
    // Check if the actual target clicked was an LI
    if (event.target.tagName === 'LI') {
      document.getElementById('del-out').innerText = 'You clicked: ' + event.target.innerText;
    }
  });
</script>

title: Attrs vs Props
content:
Attributes vs Properties

What is the difference?

Attributes are what is written in the raw HTML (like src="img.jpg"). Properties are the values currently living in the JavaScript DOM object. When you change a property via JS, the DOM updates instantly.

Modifying Properties
<input type="text" id="myInput" value="Initial HTML Attribute" style="width:80%;">
<br><br>
<button onclick="changeProp()">Change DOM Property</button>

<script>
  function changeProp() {
    const input = document.getElementById('myInput');
    // Changing the property live!
    input.value = "Changed by JavaScript Property!";
    input.style.backgroundColor = "yellow";
  }
</script>

title: CSS Manipulation
content:
CSS Manipulation

Styling with JS

You can change styles directly using element.style.property (Note: CSS properties with dashes become camelCase in JS, like backgroundColor). However, adding or removing CSS classes using classList is generally the best practice.

classList API
<style>
  .highlight { background-color: #00ffb3; color: black; padding: 10px; font-weight: bold; }
</style>

<div id="myBox" style="padding:10px; border:1px solid gray;">Watch me change!</div>
<br>
<button onclick="toggleStyle()">Toggle Highlight Class</button>

<script>
  function toggleStyle() {
    const box = document.getElementById('myBox');
    // toggles the class on and off automatically!
    box.classList.toggle('highlight');
  }
</script>

title: Callbacks
content:
Callback Functions

Passing Functions

In JavaScript, functions are objects. This means you can pass a function as an argument into another function. The function that is passed in is called a Callback Function.

Callback Example
<p id="cb-out"></p>

<script>
  // Main function accepts a name AND another function to run later
  function processUserInput(name, callback) {
    let formattedName = name.toUpperCase();
    callback(formattedName);
  }
  
  // We pass this function as the callback
  processUserInput('Sanjith', function(result) {
    document.getElementById('cb-out').innerText = "Hello, " + result;
  });
</script>

title: Closures Deep Dive
content:
Closures Deep Dive

Data Privacy

Closures are commonly used to give objects data privacy. The enclosed variables are only in scope within the containing (outer) function. You can't get at the data from an outside scope except through the object's privileged methods.

Private Counter
<button onclick="myCounter()">Add 1</button>
<p id="close-out">0</p>

<script>
  const add = (function () {
    let counter = 0; // This variable is private!
    return function () { counter += 1; return counter; }
  })(); // IIFE runs once setting counter to 0, and returns the inner function

  function myCounter() {
    // The global scope cannot access 'counter', but 'add' can!
    document.getElementById('close-out').innerHTML = add();
  }
</script>

title: JS Modules
content:
JavaScript Modules

Splitting Code

As your application grows, you want to split it into multiple files. Modules allow you to break your code into separate files and export and import variables and functions between them.



Module Syntax (Conceptual)
<!-- To use modules in HTML, you must set type="module" -->
<script type="module">
  // Inside math.js:
  // export const add = (x, y) => x + y;
  
  // Inside your main app.js file:
  // import { add } from './math.js';
  
  // console.log(add(5, 5));
</script>

title: Errors & Stack Trace
content:
Error Objects & Stack Trace

Debugging Code

When an error occurs, JavaScript creates an Error object. This object contains a name, a message, and a stack property which tells you exactly which line of code triggered the crash.

Reading the Stack
<p id="err-stack" style="font-family:monospace; color:#ef4444; font-size:12px;"></p>

<script>
  try {
    // Intentionally throwing a custom error
    throw new Error('Database connection failed!');
  } catch (err) {
    document.getElementById('err-stack').innerHTML = 
      "<b>Type:</b> " + err.name + "<br>" +
      "<b>Msg:</b> " + err.message + "<br>" +
      "<b>Stack:</b><br>" + err.stack.replace(/\n/g, '<br>');
  }
</script>

title: Timers
content:
JavaScript Timers

Delaying Execution

The window object allows execution of code at specified time intervals. setTimeout() executes a function once after a delay. setInterval() executes a function continuously at the specified interval.

Live Timer
<h3 id="clock">0</h3>
<button onclick="stopClock()">Stop Timer</button>

<script>
  let seconds = 0;
  
  // Run this function every 1000ms (1 second)
  const intervalId = setInterval(() => {
    seconds++;
    document.getElementById('clock').innerText = seconds + " seconds passed.";
  }, 1000);
  
  // Function to stop the interval using the ID
  function stopClock() {
    clearInterval(intervalId);
    document.getElementById('clock').innerText += " (Stopped)";
  }
</script>

title: Promise Chaining
content:
Promise Chaining

Handling Async Sequences

Sometimes you need to perform several asynchronous operations in a row, where one operation depends on the result of the previous one. This is done by chaining `.then()` methods.

Chaining API Requests
<p id="chain-out">Fetching data...</p>

<script>
  fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1')
    .then(response => response.json()) // Step 1: Parse JSON
    .then(data => {                    // Step 2: Use Data
      document.getElementById('chain-out').innerText = 'User: ' + data.name;
      // Return a new promise for the next .then()
      return fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts?userId=' + data.id);
    })
    .then(response => response.json()) // Step 3: Parse 2nd request
    .then(posts => {                   // Step 4: Use 2nd Data
      document.getElementById('chain-out').innerText += '\nHas ' + posts.length + ' posts.';
    })
    .catch(err => console.error('Error in chain:', err));
</script>

title: Async Patterns
content:
Async Patterns: Async/Await

Modern Try/Catch

The async/await syntax allows you to write Promise-based code as if it were synchronous, making "Callback Hell" and complex .then() chains a thing of the past.

Async/Await API Call
<p id="aw-out">Loading...</p>

<script>
  async function fetchUser() {
    try {
      const res = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/2');
      if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Network failed");
      
      const data = await res.json();
      document.getElementById('aw-out').innerText = 'Found: ' + data.name;
    } catch(err) {
      document.getElementById('aw-out').innerText = 'Error: ' + err.message;
    }
  }
  
  fetchUser();
</script>

title: Fetch Options
content:
Fetch API Options

Sending Data (POST)

By default, fetch() makes a GET request (reading data). To send data to a server (like submitting a form), you must configure the Fetch options to use a POST method, add Headers, and Stringify your Body.

POST Request
<button onclick="sendData()">Send POST Request</button>
<p id="post-out"></p>

<script>
  async function sendData() {
    const newPost = { title: 'My Fetch', body: 'Learning JS', userId: 1 };
    
    const res = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts', {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify(newPost)
    });
    
    const data = await res.json();
    // The API responds with the ID of the newly created post!
    document.getElementById('post-out').innerText = 'Success! Created Post ID: ' + data.id;
  }
</script>

title: Web APIs
content:
Browser Web APIs

Browser Superpowers

JavaScript isn't just the core language; it is the bridge to powerful Browser APIs. These APIs are built into your web browser and allow you to interact with the device hardware and software.



  • Geolocation API: Access the user's GPS coordinates.
  • Canvas API: Draw high-performance 2D and 3D graphics.
  • Notifications API: Send system-level push notifications.
  • Web Audio API: Synthesize and manipulate audio in real-time.

title: Storage APIs
content:
Local & Session Storage

Persisting Data

The Web Storage API allows you to store key/value pairs in the browser securely. localStorage saves data permanently (until manually cleared), while sessionStorage clears immediately when the browser tab is closed.

Storage Example
<script>
  // Persists forever
  localStorage.setItem('username', 'Sanjith_Dev');
  
  // Disappears when tab closes
  sessionStorage.setItem('tempToken', 'xyz123abc');
  
  // Retrieving the data
  const user = localStorage.getItem('username');
  console.log("Welcome back, " + user);
  
  // To delete data:
  // sessionStorage.removeItem('tempToken');
  // localStorage.clear();
</script>

title: Advanced Events
content:
Advanced Browser Events

Monitoring the Window

Events aren't just for buttons! You can listen to the global window object to track user behavior like scrolling, resizing the browser, or copying text.

Window Resize Tracker
<h3>Resize your browser window!</h3>
<p id="size-out" style="font-size:24px; color:#f59e0b; font-weight:bold;"></p>

<script>
  window.addEventListener('resize', () => {
    let w = window.innerWidth;
    let h = window.innerHeight;
    document.getElementById('size-out').innerText = `Width: ${w}px, Height: ${h}px`;
  });
</script>

title: Security
content:
JavaScript Security

Protecting Your App

Because JavaScript executes directly on the client's machine, it is highly vulnerable to injection attacks. The most common is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting).

  • Sanitize Inputs: Never trust user input. Always escape HTML tags before rendering user data to the DOM via innerHTML.
  • Use Content Security Policy (CSP): A HTTP header that restricts where scripts can be loaded from.
  • Never Store Secrets: Never put API Keys, passwords, or database credentials in client-side JavaScript. They are visible to anyone who opens Developer Tools!

title: Performance
content:
Performance Tips

Optimizing the Engine

Bad JavaScript will freeze the browser and ruin the user experience. Follow these rules to keep your app running at 60 Frames Per Second.



  • Minimize DOM Access: Touching the DOM is the slowest operation in JS. Save DOM elements to variables instead of querying them inside a loop.
  • Debounce Events: If listening to `scroll` or `resize`, use a Debounce function so your code doesn't fire 1,000 times a second.
  • Avoid Reflows: Changing layout styles (like `width` or `margin`) forces the browser to recalculate the whole page. Change `transform` or `opacity` instead.

title: Ultimate Summary
content:
Course Completed & Career Roadmap 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the ultimate JavaScript Masterclass. You now possess the programming logic, API integration skills, and DOM manipulation mastery required to build professional web applications.

  • Core: Syntax, Functions, ES6 Features, Arrays, Objects.
  • The Browser: DOM Traversal, Events, Storage, APIs.
  • Architecture: Promises, Async/Await, Fetch, Modules, and Closures.

Your Career Roadmap: What's Next?

You now know HTML, CSS, SQL, and JavaScript. You are officially ready to step into Full-Stack Development. Your next steps are:

  • Frontend Frameworks: Learn React, Vue, or Angular to build massive UIs efficiently.
  • Backend Environments: Learn Node.js to run JavaScript on the server, connecting directly to your SQL databases.
  • Build Projects: Stop following tutorials and start building your own ideas! Build a weather app, a task manager, or an e-commerce clone to build your portfolio.

Welcome to the world of Software Engineering!
